title: Alex Reynolds -Hiring Manger Interview - Operations | Account Director
meeting_title: Alex Reynolds -Hiring Manger Interview - Operations | Account Director

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi, Andi, how are you?
All right, how are you?
I'm doing well.
Where are you calling from?
I am in South Carolina, how about you?
I am in Rhode Island.
Awesome, yes, good afternoon, East Coast.
Yeah, I guess it's afternoon on West Coast too by now, but fair enough.
Cool, well, this should be pretty quick, pretty quick interview, hopefully won't take the full 45 minutes.

I can tell you a little bit about the role and how we're thinking about it, and then we'll talk about you, and then I have like a list of three like topic segments that I want to cover in terms of questions, and then I'll make sure that you have time to ask me questions.
Cool.
So I know you talked to Tucker, just to give you a little context about how I'm thinking about the director.
We are an agency model, like we operate on an agency model, mainly organic content for blogs.
We're doing some like newsletters and social thought leadership stuff, but like, really building out like blog content for clients.
We are on the, like internally, we're, everything that we do is built on a platform that will eventually become an external facing product.
So we're building AI workflows.
If you're looking for a comparison, we started building these workflows in AirOps and then decided that we wanted to have more options for customization.
And so that's kind of like where the basis of.

Our product, so kind of similar idea on the workflow front, but today, like the way that we're, like clients come to us for the full end-to-end content production, including like publishing to their site.
So we work in a pod model, which should be very familiar to anybody that's ever worked in an agency.

So we have managing editors and content managers who are generating content in the workflows on our platform, and then we have account strategists who are leading the, like day-to-day project management, thinking about like tracking towards goal, alignment with like contractual obligations, things like that.
And then the account director is really a high-level strategist and relationship builder for the accounts.
So account strategists and managing editors will manage kind of books of like three to five clients.
And then you would manage probably two to three pods of account strategists with managing editors.
So that's what that looks like.
Any questions?
No, not yet.
No, keep going.
Okay, cool.
Well, that was it.
So that was like just like the high level of review.
I thought it would be useful to see like kind of where this person fits in the org.
But yeah, we'd love to hear a little bit about you and your experience.
Well, yeah, the role I'm in now is very similar to what you just described.
I'm laying a pod.
Our pods are structured.
Sounds a little bit different.
Whereas we have a little bit.
Larger pods and about eight accounts per pod.
So I'm overseeing, I'm managing four of the accounts in my pod and then overseeing like the overall direction of the pod.
We kind of treat every pod as like a mini business.
So we're even looking at labor costs, earnings before tax and stuff like that on a monthly basis, just to make sure we're actually, you know, driving profit for the business as a whole.
High level strategy and building relationships is absolutely my biggest focus, or at least ideally my biggest focus on a day-to-day basis.
Obviously, there's times where someone on my team either isn't equipped for a task or needs more support or something just isn't going to plan or needs to be done, know, turned around super quickly.
So, you know, I'm happy to be hands-on as well.
I actually started at Graphite as an account manager three years ago.
So I've done pretty much every step of the SEO process.
Um, we do, uh, we do a ton of, a ton of blog strategy.
So really well-versed in film realizing myself.

With a new client, starting to understand stakeholders, what are the actual clients' priorities, and what's driving impact for their business currently, so that we can actually build a functional strategy that's going to drive impact for them and not just drive a bunch of clicks.
We do a lot more than blog strategy, too.
We do a lot of programmatic, CRO.
Obviously, we're doing a lot of AEO now, which is just like kind of building on all the other things we were doing.

I've been doing – I've been working on AEO strategies for – strongly for like the past 12 months with Webflow, starting with a few – that's the only client I can really name publicly because they're super public about our work.
But we're working on it with all of our clients.

Well, Webflow was a really early adopter for us where they gave us the resources to test and really lean into AEO, and now we're really seeing the benefit where I think like 40% of our non-brand signups are coming from – mostly ChatGBT.
Yeah.
And that's just been an explosion since January.
January was kind of where we saw like the threshold of like, oh, this can actually drive incremental impact.
And now it's driving over 40% of our non-brand signups, which is awesome.
We're obviously like most of our clients aren't seeing that much impact from ChatGPT yet, where we can lean in and put that many resources towards it.
But they're a really, really cool case where we've been able to get a lot of learning.
So like yesterday, like Atlas released.
So like all our clients are just like asking, what do we need to do for Atlas, you know, to optimize for Atlas?
I'm like, well, let's pump the brakes a little bit because we don't know even if it's going to drive any traffic for us or, you know, how they're setting things up.
But at the same time, kind of have to know enough where we can speak about it intelligently.
So we're talking, you know, I had a client literally asking yesterday already about, you know, what do we need for accessibility?
So we were talking about some of the ARIA tags in HTML and things that we can do that may or
may not be impactful, but right now that, you know, Atlas is at least saying in their documentation that will be impactful.
I saw today, actually, that their search engine is powered by Google.
So that's really interesting.
So a lot of the work should translate if Atlas is adopted.
Yeah, yeah.
It's so frustrating that we call our internal platform Atlas.
So we're going to have to think of a new name.
That is terrible.
Like, stop it.
All the new names releasing.
Yeah.
Just got, like, ChatGPTWeb or something.
Yeah.
Cool.
That was super helpful.
Sounds like, yeah, a lot of what you're doing is aligned with what we're looking for here.
Do you say you manage, like, six to eight clients?
So our pod oversees eight clients.
I personally...
So I'm hands-on with four of them.
And then we actually have two directors in a pod.
And that's partially because our scopes are really large.
So they're kind of relatively open-ended where we just do as much as we can for our clients.
So if we were like in the past, we did smaller scopes and I was overseeing like five to six.
But now we're really focused on large, large open-ended scopes.
So directors are, for the most part, limited at four unless there's like a special situation.
So I oversee four and then our pod is eight.
So I, all the accounts I work on have an account manager, a junior account manager, and then a content specialist.
And they're the ones actually like doing the topic research, making content briefs, topics lists, things like that.
The junior account manager really oversees like the editorial strategy.
Account manager is ideally like owning the day-to-day, making sure everything is running smoothly, and then even owning a lot of the client-facing communication as well.
And that way the director is laddering to exactly.
Exactly what you said.
High-level strategy, making sure we're thinking three, six months down the road, building the relationships that are going to help us come renewal time.
That's my primary KPI right now is driving renewals because we have a good pipeline, but it's always just more profitable to renew a client rather than to onboard a new one.
So Graphite is also a little unique in that we have direct reports, but they're not necessarily the people that we work with on our accounts.
So the account managers on my accounts actually don't directly report to me right now, but I do have four direct reports, and then one of them has a direct report also, so I oversee five people.
And the difference there is the direct reports.
When we're meeting weekly or biweekly, our conversations are more like, how do we take you to the next step in your growth curve?
We do biannual performance reviews for when we can get promotions.
So what are you going for in the next six months?
How do we set you up on a...
A growth curve to get you, get you there.
And then obviously, like, are you, you know, are you operating currently at what we need, where we need you at?
And if there is training or support that needs to be done there, you know, helping out with that aspect as well.
So the account managers in your, so who, what's the role of your direct reports?
Are they also account managers?
So I lied.
I have three different account managers I work with.
One of them actually is a direct report.
They recently were promoted to account manager.
So I didn't think about that.
So I have one, one account manager.
two junior account managers, and one content specialist.
And my, my, my account manager also has a content specialist that reports to them.
Okay.
Just think.
Mostly interesting for our own, like, I'm like, oh.
The same thing when do these conversations was like, how are you guys set up?
It's kind of interesting.
Because we did a huge transition, like.
Two years ago.
And we're still kind of, you know, figuring out the, you know, tweaking things and making things work.

Like we just had an all hands the other day where I think over the next six months or so, we're going to be doing pod restructuring because now we've kind of come into a spot where the distribution of talent isn't necessarily even across pods, just due to hiring or different accounts needed different people.
So that's, yeah, I think it's always kind of a tweaking the formula type of deal.
Yeah.
I think a lot about the profitability of pods and kind of how that shakes out.
How do you like think about and prioritize your work with that in mind?
Or just like how are you prioritizing your week across like four clients with endless scope?
Yeah.
So our, kind of one of graphite's like big mottos is like 90, 95% of like typical SEO work doesn't really drive in.
It's more just files into best practices, and it's really the 5% that works.
And you can really apply that methodology to anything.
I'm probably at fault for applying it to too many things in my life, especially outside of work.

But in the sense of how we're thinking about our engagement, it's like typically we have usually two primary KPIs, one top of funnel, usually non-brand clicks, and then some type of ideally revenue-driving KPIs.
Sometimes clients don't want us to get to the revenue aspect because another team owns a sales funnel or something like that, and they just want clear ownership.
So sometimes it's like a sign up a step earlier, but some type of bottom funnel conversion.

So always think about things through the lens of what are driving those KPIs, and it is typically like if we make a list of like 20 things we want to work on, there's like three things that are really going to move the needle.
So that's how we focus on a project-to-project basis.
I'm lucky enough now where all my projects are actually at the same, they have the same monthly fee.
So I don't have one that's a winner, and I know I need to prioritize them over the other accounts.
So what really happens is what's going on with the account right now?
Are we heading towards a renewal?
Are we heading towards a busy season?
Is the C-suite asking questions, and we need to make sure we're hitting our goals over the next month?
So there's a lot of nuance into where I'm spending more time on.
So for example, I kicked off a new account two weeks ago.

So right now, that's taking probably like 60% of my time, just navigating a new org, building relationships with stakeholders, laying the groundwork for the initial strategy, and making sure we're driving impact in like the first 30 to 60 days.
Once that kind of smooths over, it'll kind of be, I have another project that's renewing in February.
So that's right now, I'm scheduling meetings with leadership to figure out how you guys are thinking about budget for 2026.
Is graphite on your mind?
Are we aligned on roadmap for the potential next year?
And then we'll kind of gear up to doing a big...
Renewal pitch over the next few months.
So I have had times where I've had a certain client that they're paying us a lot more, so we have to prioritize them.
Right now I'm not in that situation, so it's just a lot of nuance of what are the different needs across my accounts.
Yeah.
Can you give me an example of a time where you were responsible for driving a strategic initiative or an internal project, and you had to define the path forward yourself?
So this question is really about like cross-functional alignment and creating, you know, certainty when there's from ambiguity.
Are you thinking specifically internally or with a...
With a client?
Internally.
Cool.
I think, so, I think you have two examples.
I don't know if either of them, like, directly answer your question.
But one, I think, is, like, we do this a lot when people, like, they have a goal for a promotion.
So we, you know, I refer back to what are our guidelines and our benchmarks for each account role, because we do have those specified.
And then I kind of build a plan to ladder towards them.
And it's usually a plan over at least six months.
And we're specifically saying, okay, we know what a project is kicking off in a few months.
I want to get you more involved so you can kick off a strategy from the beginning.
Or, you we set up those shadowing sessions as the thing's set up.
And then we meet usually on, like, a monthly basis to make sure we're tracking towards our goals.
Specifically, like, cross-functionally, we've had, so, Graphites, we do editorial as well in-house.
So we have a full team of editors and they have their own management system.
And it's actually pretty separate from the account team to where actually like a year ago, they've all come under the same roof now.
But like a year ago, they're actually like technically a separate business called Apostrophe.
That at times has caused a lot of disconnect, whereas like the editorial folks aren't really looped into the strategy.
They're just really focused on their task.
They have a really strict set of rules where it's on the account side where, like I mentioned, we're very open-ended and we're very flexible based on our clients' needs.
So for example, editorial, like they typically like to work on like a 1,500 word limit for an article.
Well, from the account side, we more look at it as what are the competitors doing?
What's ranking right now in the top of the SERP?
And if that's, you know, if we need to write 2,000 words to cover that amount of content or 1,000 words, it doesn't really matter.
But we want to make sure we're just going to do whatever's needed.
So we've had to.
Go through a lot of processes and alignment with the editorial team specifically, and our pod specifically, because we had so many clients that had unique work cases.
So we did some training both with our team, because my biggest thing is always control what you can control.
Make sure we're not just getting flustered by other people, not doing their things, and make sure we're still making sure we're crossing all our T's and dot R's.
So we did a lot of training internally about how we can collaborate cross-functionally.
With a positive attitude, making sure we're setting other people up for success and not just being worried about our task, maintaining ownership of a task all the way through completion.
So say, like, I wrote a content brief.
My job doesn't end when I pass it over to editorial.
I should continue to follow up with editorial.
I should even make sure it actually gets published, or if I may or may not be responsible for that.
And actually look into the impact, too, because that helps you realize the work you're doing is actually driving impact.
And then we as.

So we did that on our pod, and then myself and the other director of my pod, we had several meetings with editorial leadership, just so they could, you we could be just transparent about the frustrations on our end, they could be transparent about the frustrations on their end, and then we had really productive conversations about, hey, we can include stopgaps earlier on, so that we can, when these, you know, different situations arise, can just align on them, within the correct stakeholders and make a decision.

We implemented, like, a new editorial kickoff process at the start of every engagement, so that we can hopefully get a head start on if there are going to be different nuances to a project, we can better communicate that to editorial.
And that's been really, really helpful.
Like, editorial still has the rules that they have to go by, because they have to pay writers a certain amount, based on what they're writing.
So, like, they have, you know, very tangible bandwidth, or rails that they have to operate within.
But we've better realized, like, how to operate with them, and how to, you know...
Flag when things are going to be different.

And the good thing is that we've trained our junior account managers and content specialists to do that so that now leadership really isn't brought in unless it's like, we really need to just give sign off on something.
That's awesome.
Okay, last questions are going to be more about your management experience.
So I know you don't manage, you're not like the direct manager for the pod, for everyone in your pod, but how do you guys divide work across the team who divides it out and thinks about that?
So it's definitely me and the other director in the pod, we meet once a month, just her and I, we have like our own like pod capacity working, like working.

Doc, whatever information we're getting from sales of when they think we're going to get new projects, or if we think somebody's going to renew or not renew, we kind of work that all in, and we kind of sketch out a plan.

Then we also meet monthly with leadership and sales, and then we speak with them about capacity as well, and that's where if we think we need to hire somebody, or we need to borrow somebody from another pod, or something's just not working out within our team, and we need to make structure changes, that's where we have those conversations.

So we think about this a lot, because our goal is, because A, it's consulting, and you just have weeks that go crazy, and you can't anticipate it, so we want to keep everybody at about an 80% bandwidth level, so that way we have the opportunity, A, they just have the opportunity to do the work and do it at a really high level, because premium is essential for us, and then B, when we do have those weeks, or days, or whatever, when we need extra, people have the capacity for it.
Because us at the director level, we're definitely fine if we need to pull late hours.
But we try not to put that on our team.
We don't want that to be part of the culture that you need to do that, even though it is consulting, it does happen sometimes.
Then on a more week-to-week basis, we have a lot of one-on-ones.
I have one-on-ones with my reports, either weekly or bi-weekly.
It just depends.
My content specialists, we meet bi-weekly because they're pretty much just working on content briefs.
Sometimes we loop them into extra projects, but it's more just like making sure things are going.
My junior account managers and account managers, we definitely meet weekly, both to make sure they're prioritizing things across their accounts.
And I'm helping them delegate within the team as well.
And then just to also make sure we're staying on track for the account strategy-wise and things like that.
So we also have a pod meeting once a week where everybody just kind of gives their priorities for the week.
And that's an easy way for us to, again, make sure everybody...
It's kind of focused on like the two or three most important things that are going to drive impact for whatever their goal is for that point in time.
So there's a lot of like medium planning.
We can't really do a lot of long-term planning just due to the nature of consulting.
Like we don't really know what's coming down in the pipeline like six or 12 months down the road.
But we do a lot of like next quarter and the quarter after that planning.
And then like in the end, and of course the immediate term.
We're just meeting a lot with our team.
Every time I meet with somebody, I'm just stressing the importance of them communicating their bandwidth.
And like, and just stressing that like part of a remote environment is that we don't have direct visibility into what you're doing.
So we need that to make sure, you know, you're set up for success.
But then also I can set up the entire team for success as well.
Yeah.
Uh, that's definitely something we struggle with too.
Um, Mm-hmm.
Mm Mm-hmm.-hmm.
We, yeah, we have a team that's all over the globe.
And we, there are a lot of people where we're like, okay, we think this person has capacity, like, and then it turns out that they've been like helping somewhere else and not telling anybody.
And it's like, okay, well, we can't plan resourcing if, like, it's actually taking two people to manage this account and, like, your friend just tagged you in a DM and you decided to help out.
And, like, I think it's one of the things I like about the team is how willing that they are to jump in.
But it makes, like, planning when somebody has to go out for a family emergency or something, like, pulling those resources can sometimes be difficult in terms of visibility.
So, a lot of what you said makes a lot of sense.
What has been your biggest mistake as a manager?
As a manager?
Account Manager.
So I think it ladders into like just what we were just talking about.
I can be very intense.
And I came like started as an account manager.
And one of the things that made me very successful as an account manager is that I could take on large workloads and turn them around and still be premium quality and drive impact.
That allowed me to grow really quickly.
And I was already managing as an account manager, but like still, I was owning a lot of the tactical work.
As I got more into senior manager positions, and especially in the director role, I quickly realized that what I agree to like has a really strong trickle down effect on my team.
So I can't always just operate at the intensity as if like myself were the account manager.
So there was definitely a few a few periods where I unintentionally inundated my team.
And what I directly saw was actually like the team was more than willing to step up to the plate.
That's another cool thing that we deal with is it's very cool is everybody just wants to say yes, and they want new opportunities.
But the flip side of that is they can get underwater, put themselves underwater.
So I intentionally did that to my team.
And like I said, luckily, nobody was really disgruntled, but I directly noticed a decrease in the quality of work that was being done.

And actually an increase in that ended up being more time for me because I was having to go back and get things to the level we actually needed them to, spend more time with people to correct mistakes, make sure they weren't happening again.
So that's why I'm very intentional about all the things I just said to make sure we are setting people up for success and really just agreeing.
Like I don't do a lot of pushing back on clients to be transparent.
Like I can be a yes man at times.
But what I always do is like, yes, we can do that.
But this is how it's going to affect our roadmap.
This is the impact I think is going to drive so that I'm kind of making it clear how we should how we should prioritize.
And what ends up usually happening is, like, we really keep our strategies limited to things that are really, really driving impact and that we can scale.
And that kind of helps the team stay focused as well.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, those are all the questions I had.
What questions do you have for me?
Well, said a bit about the role, which was good context.
Thank you.
I think one thing you said was interesting is that, like, you guys are, everything you're doing is, building AI workflows, and the goal is for that to be a consumer-facing product.
We kind of, like, we do, has done something similar at Graphite, where, like, we also build our own tools.
I don't think they're as advanced on AI workflows as you guys, because we've been doing it, like, in the past.
So we're kind of already built on older systems.
But we are now building new AI workflows.
Anyway, the point of that was, there was a point where the goal was that for it to be.

So I guess just the question, the real question I want to ask is how do, and maybe Marcel is the best person to ask this, but for the long-term vision of the business, how do we see the consulting and the consumer-facing SaaS product kind of interacting?

Is it still going to be like the service as a software type of deal where we're making this stuff consumer-facing, but we still have like a consulting team leading the project that might be a little bit thinner?
Or do we think like the consulting is going to get phased out?
Do you know?

Yeah, I think we see it as a way to split off clients who are outside of the ICP for like the agency consulting model, and who, yeah, who either don't have the resources, like don't have the appetite to hire somebody to like...
We also have some clients that really want the visibility, transparency, and to be super hands-on.
They're excited about the workflows themselves.
So they might still pay us to run everything, or they might leverage the consulting arm to scale up the work, but they want to be in the system.
Like, there's so much to say about that needs to still be done about, like, the interface and making sure that, like, all of the tweaks that need to be made.

Like, sometimes it is a fact that when the templates, like, when the pipelines are built, and that, like, the template of that workflow that we pull from isn't going to be enough to get, like, a super technical client over the line in terms of production.
And, I mean, it still only gets us, like, 80, 90% of the way there, even when it's really good.
But, like, sometimes it's just going to be garbage because...
Like a client is like in a cutting edge field or something like that.

So we need to like make it easy enough to change like the structure and function of the workflows that we have for a client to like own it on their own, which I think is one of the cool things about the way that we're operating that like we are our own guinea pigs.
But it does like I think there will always be like the agency piece and for I think a very long time in the future.

It's just that like we're disqualifying so many like clients right now that just aren't in the IP of like we know that we can like build and get you results, but they want like the tools and they want to know how to do it.
And so we would love to them to like build on their own in our workspace.
Well, that makes a lot of sense.
And I do think it's really cool that you guys are building.
the workflows also for the for the team to use.
I mean, it's, it's really, really important.
And it was, after Tucker initially reached out, I hadn't heard of growthx before.
And just like transparently, was like, Oh, growthx AI agency, what is this going to be?
And the more I looked into it, was like, Oh, this is actually really interesting.
And I've, you know, these, I have these conversations every every once in a while, and, you know, somebody interesting reaches out on LinkedIn.
And it seems like most of the leaders that I've talked to of agencies, like, they see their agency going in the direction where it's mostly AI powered with, you know, a smaller team, ideally.
And I, so I think it's really cool that you guys kind of are like building that from the ground up, instead of having to transition like a traditional agent agency to that.
So that's something that was, that's pretty exciting to me.
I guess, another question about the pods.
So just to confirm, like one pod is basically Okay.
Strategists, like an account manager, a content strategist, and then an editor?
Is that what it was?
So there are managing editors and there are content managers.
We're doing a little restructuring ourselves right now.
So like there is one or the other in the pod just based on like when they were hired.
And then like an account strategist.

Um, so like, and, and then you would manage like two to three account, like strategists that are managing a couple of accounts, um, with those like content man, like somebody generating the content underneath them.
Gotcha.
And then, uh, like typically how many accounts does each, each pod own?
So the account strategists, I think we'll own like five, um, longer term, um, three to five is kind of where they are today.
Um, and then the, like.
The managing editor slash content manager who's in the platform, it will own like two to three.
Okay.
And then, so those roles are already in place, right?
And they're like, they're already operating.
And then, so the, but the account director role is new?
No, that's also still operating.
We had, we hired a bunch of, or like a batch of directors in, I think like May.
And then we're kind of operating and stabilizing the pod model.
And now we're, we've grown really fast and now we need another pod.
Very cool.
Okay.
Awesome.
How are the, I guess, I don't know if you, you want to answer this question personally.
How are the account strategists?
Like that's the biggest, the hardest role at Graphite for us to, to fill.
Yeah.
I think I've done 75 interviews in the last like two months.
on these.
So I agree.
I think we think we've really honed in on the right profile.
But yes, it's taken us quite some time to, to fill with the right people.
I, I think the thing that we're trying to do is figure out how much SEO specific and like strategy experience this person needs to have, versus like kind of CS ish background.
And there's, you know, having both being really strong at both of those, like, is relatively rare.
So I think we're leaning towards, that's why we're shifting, like, everybody at the base level of the pod was a content manager, we're kind of, uh,
And upping that, like, the sort of baseline qualifications for that to be, like, much stronger editorially and to have some SEO experience.
So more, like, entry-level SEO and editorial experience.
And then the account strategist to be, like, a project manager and CS person.
And then, like, the director to be, like, that super strong.
Yeah, exactly.
Cool.
Do you find typically, or do you feel like typically your directors are able to focus on the high-level strategy and relationship building?
I think it's hard to do it with all of your accounts at once.
So, like, sometimes you'll hear that, like, you know, like, you have to let something drop.
One of the directors said to me, like, Monday that they're always trying to figure out, like, what's rubber and what's glass, right?
And that will change.
So, you know, we're just at one year in operation.
So we're doing our first renewals.
So like our most tenured director has like five renewals this month and is like, okay, everything is like super important.
And like, I'm being looked at like with a magnifying glass and it feels a little tough.
But I think, yes, mostly like they don't have to get too in the weeds too often so that to get like pulled in and not be able to see that like high level strategic view.

Well, not that I like, like I've said, I do that on a weekly, I actually, after I hop off this call, I'm going to be working on a technical audit, just because one of my account managers was didn't have the bandwidth for it this week.
So I'm completely with that.
But yeah, when we start talking about like 15 accounts, that's when I start to say, okay, the bandwidth could really add up.
If, if you really need to be hands on with all of these.
Yeah.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Screening interview for the Account Director, Operations role.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=267.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Strong Fit:** Alex's current role at Graphite is nearly identical, managing 4 clients and overseeing a pod of 8, with a primary focus on renewals and high-level strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=469.0)
  - [**Proven Results:** Drove significant impact, including a 40% increase in non-brand signups for Webflow via AEO (AI Engine Optimization) and successfully resolved a major cross-functional conflict.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=557.0)
  - [**Aligned Philosophy:** Prioritizes the "5% that works" (high-impact tasks) and maintains an 80% team bandwidth target to create capacity for urgent client needs.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
  - [**Excited by Model:** Strongly interested in GrowthX's AI-native agency model and its plan to spin off the internal platform into a SaaS product for clients outside the ICP.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1824.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's Agency & SaaS Model

  - [**Core Business:** Organic content agency (blogs, newsletters, social).](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=310.0)
  - [**Internal Platform:** An AI workflow tool, built in-house after outgrowing AirOps, that will eventually become a client-facing SaaS product.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=329.0)
  - [**Pod Structure:**](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=377.0)
      - [**Account Director (This Role):** High-level strategist and relationship builder.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=412.0)
      - [**Account Strategist:** Day-to-day PM, goal tracking, contractual alignment.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=377.0)
      - [**Managing Editor / Content Manager:** Generates content in the platform.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=377.0)
  - [**Reporting:** The Account Director manages 2–3 pods, each with an Account Strategist and a content creator.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=412.0)

### Alex's Experience at Graphite

  - [**Current Role:** Account Director managing 4 clients and overseeing an 8-client pod.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=469.0)
  - [**Primary Focus:** High-level strategy and relationship building, with renewals as the main KPI.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=491.0)
  - [**Strategic Approach:**](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
      - [**"90/5 Rule":** Focus on the 5% of work that drives impact, not just best practices.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
      - [**Prioritization:** Based on client needs (renewals, new kickoffs, C-suite visibility), not just account size.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1014.0)
  - [**Team Management:**](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1407.0)
      - [**Direct Reports (4):** Focus on career growth and development.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=801.0)
      - [**Pod Capacity:** Directors meet monthly to plan staffing; leadership meets with sales to align on hiring.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1407.0)
      - [**Bandwidth Target:** Keep team at \~80% capacity to create a buffer for urgent work.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1467.0)

### Strategic Leadership & Cross-Functional Alignment

  - [**AEO (AI Engine Optimization) Success:**](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=557.0)
      - [Led AEO strategy for Webflow, driving a 40% increase in non-brand signups from ChatGPT since January.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=570.0)
      - [Provides a strong foundation for advising clients on new AI models like Atlas.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=626.0)
  - [**Resolving Cross-Functional Conflict:**](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1192.0)
      - [**Problem:** A major disconnect between the flexible Account team and the rigid Editorial team (e.g., fixed 1,500-word article limit).](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1192.0)
      - [**Solution:**](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1260.0)
          - [Trained the pod on positive collaboration and end-to-end ownership.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1260.0)
          - [Met with Editorial leadership to align on processes.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1319.0)
          - [Implemented a new editorial kickoff to flag project nuances early.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1349.0)
      - [**Result:** A more collaborative workflow, with junior staff now empowered to resolve issues.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1383.0)

### Management Philosophy & Lessons Learned

  - [**Biggest Mistake:** Overloading the team with work, which unintentionally reduced quality and increased Alex's own workload for corrections.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1675.0)
  - [**Lesson Learned:** Director decisions have a strong trickle-down effect. Now intentionally manages scope by pushing back on clients with a clear "yes, but here's the impact on the roadmap" framework.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1675.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Andi Bailey:** Schedule a follow-up interview for Alex with the Head of Customer Ops.](https://fathom.video/share/WxAU1Dz6Q_xcrmD79Jt2RnsV_RiFx4yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2475.0)
